celebration: VOWEL I LIGHTS UP — the second vowel joins the wall (A · I · o · u · e). Halfway to a lit vowel wall.

songs.title: I-I-In
songs.lyrics:
[Intro — whispered]
Ih! Ih! Ih-ih-in!
(ih… ih… ih…)

[Hook]
Ih! Ih! Ih-ih-ih!
What begins with ih?
Ih-ih-insect! Ih-ih-insect!
It's an insect! (an insect!)

[Verse 2]
Ih! Ih! Ih-ih-ih!
What begins with ih?
Ih-ih-igloo! Ih-ih-igloo!
It's an igloo! (an igloo!)

[Verse 3]
Ih! Ih! Ih-ih-ih!
What begins with ih?
Ih-ih-iguana! Ih-ih-iguana!
It's an iguana! (an iguana!)

[Verse 4]
Ih! Ih! Ih-ih-ih!
What begins with ih?
Ih-ih-inchworm! Ih-ih-inchworm!
It's an inchworm! (an inchworm!)

[Bridge]
A potato?! (NO!)
P-p-potato! Not ih!
(ih… ih… ih…)

[Outro]
Ih-ih-in! Ih-ih-in!
We are IN! (we're in!)
Ih! Ih! Ih-ih-in!
songs.sunoStyle: dark trap, 68 bpm, heavy 808 bass, sparse hi-hats, deep whisper-rap verses, kids choir chant on hook, playful spooky, minimal, clean vocals, nursery trap
songs.sunoNotes: SHORT vowel /ĭ/ as in 'in'/'sit' — the lyrics spell it 'ih' so Suno never sings the letter name 'eye'. Never 'ee'. Crisp, short, front vowel. The printed classroom letter stays 'i' — 'ih' is Suno-only respelling.

songs.title: Sit, Cat, Sit!
songs.lyrics:
[Intro — whispered]
(sit… sit… sit, cat, sit…)

[Hook]
Sit, cat, sit!
Sit, cat, sit!
Will the cat sit? (will it? will it?)
Sit, cat, SIT!

[Verse 2]
Sit, cat, sit!
Sit, cat, sit!
The cat sat in a tin! (a tin?!)
That is not the mat!

[Verse 3]
Sit, cat, sit!
Sit, cat, sit!
The cat sat in a cap! (a cap?!)
That is not the mat!

[Bridge]
The cat can sit!
The cat can sit!
It sat on the mat! (at last!)

[Outro]
Sit, cat, sit.
(good cat…)
A potato sat on the mat!
(POTATO!)
songs.sunoStyle: dark trap, 68 bpm, heavy 808 bass, sparse hi-hats, deep whisper-rap verses, kids choir chant on hook, playful spooky, minimal, clean vocals, nursery trap
songs.sunoNotes: One frame: the command 'Sit, cat, sit!' plus 'The cat sat in a ___.' — a single decodable word swaps (tin, cap, pit). The joke: the cat sits everywhere except where you point, then finally on the mat. Kids choir answers the (…?!) echoes; whisper the outro, then the potato hits loud.

spreads.artNote: A child's hand pointing firmly at the mat, the tabby cat looking away, unimpressed. The classic battle: telling a cat to sit.

spreads.text: The cat sat in a tin!
spreads.artNote: The cat has instead climbed into an empty tin, sitting proudly — NOT the mat. Contrary cat.
Hand pointing again, more insistent; the cat's tail flicks. Repetition builds the joke.

spreads.text: The cat sat in a cap!
spreads.artNote: The cat now wedged into a baseball cap, smug — still not the mat.
Hand pointing a third time, exasperated; Sam and the ant watching, holding their breath.

spreads.text: The cat sat in a pit!
spreads.artNote: The cat sitting at the bottom of a little sandpit, looking up innocently — anywhere but the mat.
AT LAST — the cat sits perfectly on the mat, prim and proud, as if it had meant to all along.

spreads.text: It is a cat. It can sit.
spreads.artNote: Calm victory spread — the cat on the mat, Sam, Segina and the ant applauding. New words 'it' and 'in' anchored.
The potato has taken the mat and is 'sitting' where the cat should be — the class expects a cat but the gag noun is blank: they SHOUT 'POTATO' from the picture. The cat stares, betrayed.

sentences: The cat sat in a tin.
It is a cat. It can sit.
The pin is in the cap.
extras: 🎉 Vowel wall: the I poster LIGHTS UP — gold star added beside A (the second lit vowel)
Bingo Vowel Song ritual at the wall: 'A-E-I-O-U' with A and I now glowing
Moveable alphabet: build i-t, i-n, s-i-t, p-i-n, t-i-n, t-i-p
Sandpaper letter 'i' + sand tray — short /ĭ/, dot the i
'Sit, cat, sit!' command game — hide the cat card in a tin / cap / pit, class reads where it sat

teacherFocus: The second vowel arrives — light up the I on the vowel wall beside A and celebrate (two of five glowing, halfway to a complete wall). Teach the SHORT /ĭ/ as in 'in' and 'igloo' — dot the i, keep it quick, never 'eye'. Blend i-t, i-n, s-i-t, p-i-n so the class can suddenly read a whole new family of words. The book is one running joke — a cat that sits everywhere except where you point — so once they read line one they roar through the rest, then anchor 'it' and 'in' in the calm victory spread.
